paragraphs.text: We are floating up a steep scrubby slope.  We hear male voices gently singing "Tumbling Tumbleweeds" and a deep, affable, Western-accented voice--Sam Elliot's, perhaps:

We top the rise and the smoggy vastness of Los Angeles at twilight stretches out before us.

It is late, the supermarket all but deserted.  We are tracking in on a fortyish man in Bermuda shorts and sunglasses at the dairy case.  He is the Dude.  His rumpled look and relaxed manner suggest a man in whom casualness runs deep.
He is feeling quarts of milk for coldness and examining their expiration dates.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE-OVER
paragraphs.text: And I'm talkin' about the Dude here-- sometimes there's a man who, wal, he's the man for his time'n place, he fits right in there--and that's the Dude, in Los Angeles.
She waits, arms folded.  A small black-and white TV next to her register shows George Bush on the White House lawn with helicopter rotors spinning behind him.

The Dude, peeking over his shades, scribbles something at the little customer's lectern.  Milk beads his mustache.

paragraphs.text: ...and even if he's a lazy man, and the Dude was certainly that--quite possibly the laziest in Los Angeles County.
The Dude has his Ralph's Shopper's Club card to one side and is making out a check to Ralph's for sixty-nine cents.

His head is grabbed from behind and tucked into an armpit. We track with him as he is rushed through the living room, his arm holding the satchel flailing away from his body. Going into the bedroom the outflung satchel catches a piece of doorframe and wallboard and rips through it, leaving a hole.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hey man--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Fucking dog has papers, Dude.--Over the line!
Smokey turns from his last roll to look at Walter.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Smokey Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Over the line, Smokey!  I'm sorry. That's a foul.

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Bullshit.  Eight, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Excuse me!  Mark it zero.  Next frame.

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Bullshit. Walter!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: This is not Nam.  This is bowling. There are rules.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Come on Walter, it's just--it's Smokey.  So his toe slipped over a little, it's just a game.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: This is a league game.  This determines who enters the next round- robin, am I wrong?

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Yeah, but--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Am I wrong!?

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Yeah, but I wasn't over.  Gimme the marker, Dude,  I'm marking it an eight.
Walter takes out a gun.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Smokey my friend, you're entering a world of pain.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hey Walter--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Mark that frame an eight, you're entering a world of pain.

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: I'm not--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: A world of pain.
A manager in a bowling-shirt style uniform is running for a phone.

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Look Dude, I don't hold with this. This guy is your partner, you should--
Walter primes the gun and points it at his head.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: HAS THE WHOLE WORLD GONE CRAZY?  AM I THE ONLY ONE HERE WHO GIVES A SHIT ABOUT THE RULES?  MARK IT ZERO!
The Pomeranian is excitedly yapping at Walter's elbow, making high body-twisting tail-wagging leaps.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, they're calling the cops, put the piece away.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: MARK IT ZERO!

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: Walter--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: YOU THINK I'M FUCKING AROUND HERE? MARK IT ZERO!!

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: All right!  There it is!  It's fucking zero!
He points frantically at the score projected above the lane.

paragraphs.speaker: SMOKEY
paragraphs.text: You happy, you crazy fuck?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: This is a league game, Smokey!
Walter and the Dude walk to the Dude's car.  The Pomeranian trots happily behind Walter who totes the empty carrier.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, you can't do that.  These guys're like me, they're pacificists. Smokey was a conscientious objector.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: You know Dude, I myself dabbled with pacifism at one point.  Not in Nam, of course--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: And you know Smokey has emotional problems!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: You mean--beyond pacifism?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: He's fragile, man!  He's very fragile!
As the two men get into the car:

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Huh.  I did not know that.  Well, it's water under the bridge.  And we do enter the next round-robin, am I wrong?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: No, you're not wrong--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Am I wrong!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You're not wrong, Walter, you're just an asshole.
They watch a squad car take a squealing turn into the lot.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Okay then.  We play Quintana and O'Brien next week.  They'll be pushovers.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Just, just take it easy, Walter.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: That's your answer to everything, Dude.  And let me point out--pacifism is not--look at our current situation with that camelfucker in Iraq-- pacifism is not something to hide behind.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Well, just take 't easy, man.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I'm perfectly calm, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah?  Wavin' a gun around?!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: (smugly) Calmer than you are.
-his irritates the Dude further.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Just take it easy, man!
Walter is still smug.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Calmer than you are.
A large, brilliant Persian rug lies beneath the Dude's beat- up old furniture.
At the table next to the answering machine the Dude is mixing kalhua, rum and milk.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Dude, this is Smokey.  Look, I don't wanna be a hard-on about this, and I know it wasn't your fault, but I just thought it was fair to tell you that Gene and I will be submitting this to the League and asking them to set aside the round.  Or maybe forfeit it to us--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Shit!

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: --so, like I say, just thought, you know, fair warning.  Tell Walter.
A beep.

paragraphs.speaker: ANOTHER VOICE
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski, this is Brandt at, uh, well--at Mr. Lebowski's office. Please call us as soon as is convenient.
Beep.

paragraphs.speaker: ANOTHER VOICE
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski, this is Fred Dynarski with the Southern Cal Bowling League. I just got a, an informal report, uh, that a uh, a member of your team, uh, Walter Sobchak, drew a loaded weapon during league play--
We hear the doorbell.
It swings open to reveal a short, hairy, muscular but balding middle-aged man in a black T-shirt and black cut-off jeans.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hiya Allan.

paragraphs.speaker: ALLAN
paragraphs.text: Dude, I finally got the venue I wanted.  I'm Performing my dance quintet--you know, my cycle--at Crane Jackson's Fountain Street Theatre on Tuesday night, and I'd love it if you came and gave me notes.
The Dude takes a swig of his kalhua.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Sure Allan, I'll be there.

paragraphs.speaker: ALLAN
paragraphs.text: Dude, uh, tomorrow is already the tenth.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, yeah I know. Okay.

paragraphs.speaker: ALLAN
paragraphs.text: Just, uh, just slip the rent under my door.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, okay.
The  voice continues on the machine.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: --serious infraction, and examine your standing.  Thank you.  Beep.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski, Brandt again.  Please do call us when you get in and I'll send the limo.  Let me assure you--I hope you're not avoiding this call because of the rug, which, I assure you, is not a problem.  We need your help and, uh--well we would very much like to see you.  Thank you. It's Brandt.
We are pushing Brandt down the high-ceilinged hallway. Distantly, we hear a dolorous soprano.  Brandt talks back over

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: We've had some terrible news.  Mr. Lebowski is in seclusion in the West Wing.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Huh.

Brandt throws open a pair of heavy double doors.  The music washes over us as we enter a great study where Jeffrey Lebowski, a blanket thrown over his knees, stares hauntedly into a fire, listening to Lohengrin.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski.
Jeffrey Lebowski waves the Dude in without looking around.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: It's funny.  I can look back on a life of achievement, on challenges met, competitors bested, obstacles overcome.  I've accomplished more than most men, and without the use of my legs.  What. . . What makes a man, Mr. Lebowski?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I don't know, sir.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Is it. . . is it, being prepared to do the right thing?  Whatever the price?  Isn't that what makes a man?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Sure.  That and a pair of testicles.
Lebowski turns away from the Dude with a haunted stare, lost in thought.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: You're joking.  But perhaps you're right.
The Dude thumps at his chest pocket.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Mind if I smoke a jay?

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Bunny.
He turns back around and the firelight shows teartracks on his cheeks.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: 'Scuse me?

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Bunny Lebowski. . . She is the light of my life.  Are you surprised at my tears, sir?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Fuckin' A.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Strong men also cry. . . Strong men also cry.
He clears his throat.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: I received this fax this morning.
Brandt hastily pulls a flimsy sheet from his clipboard and hands it to the Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: As you can see, it is a ransom note. Sent by cowards.  Men who are unable to achieve on a level field of play. Men who will not sign their names. Weaklings.  Bums.

shots.heading: WE HAVE BUNNY.  GATHER ONE MILLION DOLLARS IN UNMARKED NON-
CONSECUTIVE TWENTIES.  AWAIT INSTRUCTIONS.  NO FUNNY STUFF.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Bummer.
Lebowski looks soulfully at the Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Brandt will fill you in on the details.
He wheels his chair around to once again gaze into the fire. Brandt tugs at the Dude's shirt and points him back to the hall.
The soprano's singing is once again faint.  Brandt's voice is hushed:

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski is prepared to make a generous offer to you to act as courier once we get instructions for the money.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Why me, man?

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: He suspects that the culprits might be the very people who, uh, soiled your rug, and you're in a unique position to confirm or, uh, disconfirm that suspicion.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: So he thinks it's the carpet-pissers, huh?

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Well Dude, we just don't know.
CRASH--scattered by a strike, in slow motion.

Still in slow motion.  We are looking across the length of the bowling alley at a tall, thin, Hispanic bowler displaying perfect form.  He wears an all-in-one dacron-polyester stretch bowling outfit with a racing stripe down each side.
On the Dude, sitting next to Walter in the molded plastic chairs. The Dude is staring off towards the bowler.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Fucking Quintana--that creep can roll, man--
Displaying great slow-motion form as the Dude and Walter's conversation continues over.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah, but he's a fucking pervert, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: The man is a sex offender.  With a record.  Spent six months in Chino for exposing himself to an eight- year-old.
We see Quintana, in pressed jeans and a stretchy sweater, walking up a stoop in a residential neighborhood and zinging the bell.
The VOICE-OVER conversation continues.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Huh.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: When he moved down to Venice he had to go door-to-door to tell everyone he's a pederast.
The door swings open and a beer-swilling middle-aged man looks dully out at Quintana, who looks hesitantly up.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: What's a pederast, Walter?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shut the fuck up, Donny.
scattered by a strike.
wheeling and thrusting a black gloved fist into the air.
Stitched above the breast pocket of his all-in-one is his first name, "Jesus".

shots.heading: BACK TO WALTER AND THE DUDE

paragraphs.text: They have been joined by Donny.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Anyway.  How much they offer you?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Twenty grand.  And of course I still keep the rug.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Just for making the hand-off?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah.
He slips a little black box out of his shirt pocket.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: ...They  gave  Dude  a  beeper,  so whenever these guys call--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What if it's during a game?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I told him if it was during league play--
Donny has been watching Quintana.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: If what's during league play?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Life does not stop and start at your convenience, you miserable piece of shit.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: What's wrong with Walter, Dude?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I figure it's easy money, it's all pretty harmless.  I mean she probably kidnapped herself.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: What do you mean, Dude?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Rug-peers did not do this.  I mean look at it.  Young trophy wife. Marries a guy for money but figures he isn't giving her enough.  She owes money all over town--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: That...fucking...bitch!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: It's all a goddamn fake.  Like Lenin said, look for the person who will benefit.  And you will, uh, you know, you'll, uh, you know what I'm trying to say--

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: I am the Walrus.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: That fucking bitch!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: I am the Walrus.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shut the fuck up, Donny!  V.I. Lenin! Vladimir Ilyich Ulyanov!

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: What the fuck is he talking about?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: That's fucking exactly what happened, Dude!  That makes me fucking SICK!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, well, what do you care, Walter?

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: Yeah Dude, why is Walter so pissed off?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Those rich fucks!  This whole fucking thing-- I did not watch my buddies die face down in the muck so that this fucking strumpet--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I don't see any connection to Vietnam, Walter.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Well, there isn't a literal connection, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, face it, there isn't any connection.  It's your roll.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Have it your way.  The point is--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: It's your roll--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: The fucking point is--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: It's your roll.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Are you ready to be fucked, man?
They both look up.

Quintana, on his way out, looks down at them from the lip of the lanes.  Over his polyester all-in-one he now wears a windbreaker with a racing stripe and "Jesus" stitched on the breast.  He is holding a fancy black-and-red leather ball satchel (perhaps a Sylvia Wein).  Behind him stands his partner, O'Brien, a short fat Irishman with tufted red hair.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: I see you rolled your way into the semis.  Deos mio, man.  Seamus and me, we're gonna fuck you up.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah well, that's just, ya know, like, your opinion, man.
Quintana looks at Walter.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: Let me tell you something, bendeco. You pull any your crazy shit with us, you flash a piece out on the lanes, I'll take it away from you and stick it up your ass and pull the fucking trigger til it goes "click".

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Jesus.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: You said it, man.  Nobody fucks with the Jesus.
Jesus walks away.  Walter nods sadly.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Eight-year-olds, Dude.
We are looking down at the Dude who is prone on the rug. His eyes are closed.  He wears a Walkman headset.  Leaking tinnily through the headphones we can just hear an intermittent clatter.
In his outflung hand lies a cassette case labeled VENICE
The Dude absently licks his lips as we faintly hear a hall rumbling down the lane.  On its impact with the pins, the Dude opens his eyes.
He screams.
A blonde woman looms over him.  Next to  her a  young man in paint-spattered denims stoops and swings something towards the carrier.
The sap catches the Dude on the chin and sends  his head thunking back onto the rug.
A million stars explode against a field of black.  We hear the "La-la-la-la" of The Man in Me.
The black field  dissolves into  the pattern  of the  rug. The rug rolls away to reveal an aerial view of  the city  of Los  Angeles at twilight, moving below us at great speed.
The Dude is flying over the city, his arms thrown out in front of him, the wind whipping his hair and billowing his bowling shirt. He looks up.
Ahead the mysterious blonde woman wings away, riding on the Dude's rug like a sheik on a magic carpet.  She is outpacing us, growing smaller.

The Dude does a couple of lazy crawl strokes and then notices that a bowling ball has materialized in his forward hand. His bemusement turns to concern over the aerodynamic implications just as the ball seems to suddenly assume its weight, abruptly snapping his arm down, and him after it. He is falling. From a high angle we see the Dude hurtling down toward the city, dragged by the ball.
A  reverse  looking  up shows  the Dude  hurtling toward  us out  of the inky  sky,  his eyes  wide with  horror.  Led by the bowling  ball, he zooms past the camera leaving us in black.
We hear a distant rumble, like thunder.  Dull reflections materialize in the darkness.  They are glints off the shiny surface of an oncoming bowling ball.
We pull back to reveal that the blackness was the inside of a ball return, and the gleaming bowling ball is being regurgitated up at us, overtaking us.
The Dude looks up, up, up at the looming ball, its mass rolling a huge shadow across his face.
The gleaming ball shows three dead black holes rolling toward us --finger holes.
The largest--thumb--hole rolls directly over us, engulfing us once again in black..
The black rolls away and we are spinning--spinning down a bowling lane--our point of view that of someone trapped in the thumbhole of the rolling ball.
We see the receding bowler spinning away.  It is the blonde woman, performing her follow-through.
Floor spins up at us and then away; ceiling spins up and away; the length of the alley with pins at the end; floor; ceiling; approaching pins; again and again.
We hit the pins and clatter into blackness.  We hear pins spin, hit each other and drop.
We hear an irritating, insistent beeping.

We are close on the Dude, upside down.  As the picture fades in the bowling noises continue, but filtered and faint. They come from the Dude's Walkman, the headset of which is now askew, with one arm off his ear.
As the Dude opens his eyes we spiral slowly upward to put him right side around.  His head is now resting against hardwood floor, not rug.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Oh man.
He  raises  himself  onto  his  elbows  and  massages  the red   lump  on his  jaw.  The  beeper  on his  belt is blinking red  in sync  with the continuing irritating beeps.
An  end  table  is  upset,  but  otherwise the  furniture is in place. The rug is gone.
The  Dude  looks  around.    The  bowling sounds  continue. The beeps continue.
The phone starts to jangle.
We  push  Brandt  down  the  familiar  marble  hallway. Again  there is a  distant  aria.    Brandt  throws  out a wrist to  look at  his watch.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: They called about eighty minutes ago.  They want you to take the money and drive north on the 4 5.  They'll call you on the portable phone with instructions in about forty minutes. One person only or I'd go with you. They were very clear on that: one person only.  What happened to your jaw?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Oh, nothin', you know.

They have reached the little desk outside of the big Lebowski's office; Brandt opens its bottom drawer with a key and takes out an attache case.  He hands this to the Dude along with a cellular phone in a battery-pack carrying case.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Here's the money, and the phone. Please, Dude, follow whatever instructions they give.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Uh-huh.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Her life is in your hands.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Oh, man, don't say that..

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Mr. Lebowski asked me to repeat that: Her life is in your hands.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Shit.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Her life is in your hands, Dude. And report back to us as soon as it's done.

We pan off the Dude, driving, to his point of view through the front windshield.  The headlights play over Walter standing waiting in front of the storefront of SOBCHAK SECURITY.  Though he is wearing khaki shorts and shirt, the fact that he holds a battered brown briefcase makes him look oddly like a commuter.  He also holds an irregular shape bundled in brown wrapping paper.
The car stops in front of him and he opens the Dude's door and hands in the briefcase.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Take the ringer.  I'll drive.
The Dude takes the briefcase and slides over.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: The what?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: The ringer!  The ringer, Dude!  Have they called yet?
The Dude opens the briefcase and paws bemusedly through it as the car starts rolling.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: What the hell is this?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: My dirty undies.  Laundry, Dude. The whites.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Agh--
He closes the briefcase.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, I'm sure there's a reason you brought your dirty undies--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Thaaaat's right, Dude.  The weight. The ringer can't look empty.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter--what the fuck are you thinking?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Well you're right, Dude, I got to thinking.  I got to thinking why should we settle for a measly fucking twenty grand--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: We?  What the fuck we?  You said you just wanted to come along--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: My point, Dude, is why should we settle for twenty grand when we can keep the entire million.  Am I wrong?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yes you're wrong.  This isn't a fucking game, Walter--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: It is a fucking game.  You said so yourself, Dude--she kidnapped herself--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE '
paragraphs.text: Yeah, but--
The phone chirps.  Dude grabs it.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Dude here.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: (German accent) Who is this?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Dude the Bagman.  Where do you want us to go?

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: ...Us? DUDE
Shit. . . Uh, yeah, you know, me and the driver.  I'm not handling the money and driving the car and talking on the phone all by my fucking--

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Shut the fuck up. (Beat) Hello?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah?

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Okay, listen--
Walter looks over at the Dude and bellows:

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Dude, are you fucking this up?

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Who is that?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: The driver man, I told you--
Click.  Dial tone.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Oh shit.  Walter.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What the fuck is going on there?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: They hung up, Walter!  You fucked it up!  You fucked it up!  Her life was in our hands!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Easy, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: We're screwed now!  We don't get shit and they're gonna kill her! We're fucked, Walter!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Dude, nothing is fucked.  Come on. You're being very unDude.  They'll call back.  Look, she kidnapped her--
The phone chirps.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Ya see?  Nothing is fucked up here, Dude.  Nothing is fucked.  These guys are fucking amateurs--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Shutup, Walter!  Don't fucking say peep when I'm doing business here.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: (patronizing) Okay Dude.  Have it your way.
The Dude unclips the phone from the battery pack.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: But they're amateurs.
The Dude glares at Walter.  Into the phone:

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Dude here.

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: Okay, vee proceed.  But only if there is no funny stuff.

The Dude is walking down a long corridor dressed as a cable repairman.  The Dude's face is washed with a brilliant light as the corridor opens onto a gleaming bowling alley.
In the center of the alley stands Maude Lebowski, singing operatic harmony to the Kenny Rogers song.  She wears an armored breastplate and Norse headgear, has braided pigtails, and holds a trident.
The Dude stands behind her and, pressed up against her, helps her with her follow-through as she releases a bowling ball.
The lane is straddled by a line of chorines in spangly mini- skirts, their arms akimbo, Busby-Berkley style, their legs turning the lane into a tunnel leading to the pins at the end.

But it is no longer a bowling ball rolling between their legs--it is the Dude himself, levitating inches off the lane, the tools from his utility belt swinging free.  He is face down, his arms, torpedolike, pressed against his sides.
His point of view shows the lane rushing by below, the little ball-guide arrows zipping by.
The Dude twists his body around, performing a barrel-roll so that he is now gliding along the lane face-up.
Now his point of view looks up the dresses of the passing chorines.
The Dude smiles dreamily and does a backstroke motion so that he is once again gliding face-down.  He looks forward and his forward momentum blows back his hair.
Coming at us, as we go through the last few pairs of legs, are the approaching pins.  We hit the pins, scattering them, and rush on into black.
A body drops down into the blackness in slow motion--a topless woman, squealing, her legs kicking.

As she drops out of frame, leaving blackness again, three men are entering from the background, emerging into a pool of light.  It is the Germans, advancing ominously, wielding oversized shears which they menacingly scissor.
The Dude, now standing in a field of black, reacts to the advancing Germans.  He turns and runs, fists pumping.

The scissoring sound of the shears turns into the whoosh of car-bys.  The field of black is punctured by headlights. The Dude is running blearily down the middle of the Pacific Coast Highway. Cars rush by on either side, horns blaring.

paragraphs.text: Brother Shamus?  Like an Irish monk?

paragraphs.speaker: MAN
paragraphs.text: Irish m--What the fuck are you talking about?  My name's Da Fino!  I'm a private snoop!  Like you, man!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I'm not a--ah, fuck it, just stay away from my fucking lady friend, man.

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: Hey hey, I'm not messing with your special lady--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: She's not my special lady, she's my fucking lady friend.  I'm just helping her conceive, man!

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: Hey, man, I'm not--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Who're you working for?  Lebowski? Jackie Treehorn?

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: The Gundersons.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: The?  Who the fff--

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: The Gundersons.  It's a wandering daughter job.  Bunny Lebowski, man. Her real name is Fawn Gunderson. Her parents want her back.
He is fumbling in his wallet.

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: See?
The Dude looks at the picture.
It is probably a school portrait, unmistakably Bunny, but fresh-faced, much younger looking, with a corn-fed smile and straight Partridge Family hair and bangs.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Jesus fucking Christ.

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: Crazy, huh?  Ran away a year ago.
He is holding out another picture.
The Gundersons told me to show her this when I found her. The family farm.
A bleak farmhouse and silo are the only features on a flat snow-swept landscape.
Outside of Moorhead, Minnesota.  They think it'll make her homesick.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Boy.  How ya gonna keep 'em down on the farm once they seen Karl Hungus.
He hands back the picture.
She's been kidnapped, Da Fino.  Or maybe not, but she's definitely not around.

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: Fuck, man!  That's terrible!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, it sucks.

paragraphs.speaker: DA FINO
paragraphs.text: Well maybe you and me could pool our resources--trade information-- professional courtesy--compeers, you know--
We hear distant yapping, growing louder with the hum of an approaching car.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, I get it.  Fuck off, Da Fino. And stay away from my special la-- from my fucking lady friend.
The Dude steps out to meet Walter's car as it pulls up, its passenger window open and the pomeranian leaning out and yapping.

Four people sit at a booth:  Dieter, Kieffer, Franz, all in black leather, and a young woman with long stringy blonde hair, wearing torn and patched jeans and a ribbed sleeveless tee-shirt, worn thin with age.  She is apparently braless, and is teutonically pale with birthmarks on her face and arms.

Notable  is  her  camera-side  leg,  which  ends in  a bandage- swaddled foot.  Dried rust-colored blood stains the tip of the bandage. The  four  are  arguing,  loudly,  in  German. They seem  very unhappy. A waitress enters with a checkpad and pen.

paragraphs.speaker: WAITRESS
paragraphs.text: You folks ready?
The German shouting stops.  Dieter looks sourly up.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: I haff lingenberry pancakes.

paragraphs.speaker: KIEFFER
paragraphs.text: Lingenberry pancakes.

paragraphs.speaker: FRANZ
paragraphs.text: Sree picks in blanket.
The woman speaks to Dieter in German.  He nods.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: Lingenberry pancakes.
Walter's eyes are on the road as he listens, driving, to the Dude, whose speech is occasionally punctuated by yaps from the back seat.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I mean we totally fucked it up, man. We fucked up his pay-off.  And got the kidnappers all pissed off, and the big Lebowski yelled at me a lot, but he didn't do anything.  Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Well it's, sometimes the cathartic, uh.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: I'm saying if he knows I'm a fuck- up, then why does he still leave me in charge of getting back his wife? Because he fucking doesn't want her back, man!  He's had enough!  He no longer digs her!  It's all a show! But then, why didn't he give a shit about his million bucks?  I mean, he knew we didn't hand off his briefcase, but he never asked for it back.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What's your point, Dude?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: His million bucks was never in it, man!  There was no money in that briefcase!  He was hoping they'd kill her!  You throw out a ringer for a ringer!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Shit yeah!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Okay, but how does all this add up to an emergency?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Huh?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I'm saying, I see what you're getting at, Dude, he kept the money, but my point is, here we are, it's shabbas, the sabbath, which I'm allowed to break only if it's a matter of life and death--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, come off it.  You're not even fucking Jewish, you're--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What the fuck are you talking about?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You're fucking Polish Catholic--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What the fuck are you talking about? I converted when I married Cynthia! Come on, Dude!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah, and you were--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: You know this!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: And you were divorced five fucking years ago.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah?  What do you think happens when you get divorced?  You turn in your library card?  Get a new driver's license?  Stop being Jewish?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: This driveway.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I'm as Jewish as fucking Tevye

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: It's just part of your whole sick Cynthia thing.  Taking care of her fucking dog.  Going to her fucking synagogue.  You're living in the fucking past.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Three thousand years of beautiful tradition, from Moses to Sandy Koufax-- YOU'RE GODDAMN RIGHT I LIVE IN THE PAST!   I--Jesus.  What the hell happened?
He is looking off as the car slows.  The Dude looks where Walter is looking.
Walter's car pulls up the drive into the foreground and he and the Dude get out.
Both are gaping off at the front lawn.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Jesus Christ.
Tire treads lead across the manicured front lawn to where a little red sports car rests with its hood crumpled into a palm trunk.

Through the French doors at its far end we can see Bunny, naked, briefly bouncing on the diving board before splashing into the illuminated pool outside.  Heavy metal music filters in from a boom box by the pool.
Brandt, approaching, stoops and straightens, stoops and straightens, picking up the discarded clothes that run the length of the hall.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: He can't see you, Dude.
We pull the Dude and Walter as they approach the doors to the great study.  Walter's dog follows, stiffly waving its tail.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Where'd she been?

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Visiting friends of hers in Palm Springs.  Just picked up and left, never bothered to tell us.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: But I guess she told Dieter.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Jesus, Dude!  He never even kidnapped her.

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: Who's this gentleman, Dude?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Who'm I?  I'm a fucking VETERAN!

paragraphs.speaker: BRANDT
paragraphs.text: You shouldn't go in there, Dude! He's very angry!
BANG--the Dude and Walter push through the double doors into--
The big Lebowski turns at the sound of the door.  His wheelchair hums as he spins it around.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: (bitterly) Well, she's back.  No thanks to you.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Where's the money, Lebowski?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: A MILLION BUCKS FROM FUCKING NEEDY LITTLE URBAN ACHIEVERS!  YOU ARE SCUM, MAN!
The dog yaps.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Who the hell is he?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I'll tell you who I am!  I'm the guy who's gonna KICK YOUR PHONY GOLDBRICKING ASS!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: We know the briefcase was empty, man.  We know you kept the million bucks yourself.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Well, you have your story, I have mine.  I say I entrusted the money to you, and you stole it.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: AS IF WE WOULD EVER DREAM OF TAKING YOUR BULLSHIT MONEY!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You thought Bunny'd been kidnapped and you could use it as a pretext to make some money disappear.  All you needed was a sap to pin it on, and you'd just met me.  You thought, hey, a deadbeat, a loser, someone the square community won't give a shit about.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Well?  Aren't you?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Well. . . yeah.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: All right, get out.  Both of you.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Look at that fucking phony, Dude! Pretending to be a fucking millionaire!

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: I said out.  Now.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Let me tell you something else. I've seen a lot of spinals, Dude, and this guy is a fake.  A fucking goldbricker.
He is crossing to Lebowski.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: This guy fucking walks.  I've never been more certain of anything in my life!

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Stay away from me, mister!
Walter reaches around from behind and hoists the big Lebowski out of the wheelchair by his armpits.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Walk, you fucking phony!
The big Lebowski waggles helplessly, his rubbery feet grazing the floor like a Raggedy Ann's.  The pomeranian gaily leaps and yaps.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Put me down, you son of a bitch!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: It's all over, man!  We call your fucking bluff!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: WALTER, FOR CHRIST'S SAKE!  HE'S CRIPPLED!  PUT HIM DOWN!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Sure, I'll put him down, Dude.  RAUSS! ACHTUNG, BABY!!
He shoves the big Lebowski forward and he crumples to the floor, weeping.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Oh, shit.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: (sobbing) You're bullies!  Cowards, both of you!
Walter is abashed.  The Big Lebowski flails about on the floor.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Oh, shit.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: He can't walk, Walter!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah, I can see that, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: You monsters!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Help me put him back in his chair.
Walter moves to comply.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shit, sorry man.

paragraphs.speaker: LEBOWSKI
paragraphs.text: Stay away from me!  You bullies! You and these women!  You won't leave a man his fucking balls!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter, you fuck!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shit, Dude, I didn't know.  I wouldn't've done it if I knew he was a fucking crybaby.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: We're sorry, man.  We're really sorry.
The Dude has picked up the Big Lebowski's plaid lap warmer and is frantically tucking it back in around his waist and batting the dog away.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: There ya go.  Sorry man.
Walter, puzzled, hands on hips, stands over the big Lebowski.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shit.  He didn't look like a spinal.
Scattered at the cut.
Each with a beer at the scoring table.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Sure you'll see some tank battles. But fighting in desert is very different from fighting in canopy jungle.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Uh-huh.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I mean 'Nam was a foot soldier's war whereas, uh, this thing should be a fucking cakewalk.  I mean I had an M16, Jacko, not an Abrams fucking tank.  Just me and Charlie, man, eyeball to eyeball.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: That's fuckin' combat.  The man in the black pyjamas, Dude.  Worthy fuckin' adversary.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: Who's in pyjamas, Walter?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shut the fuck up, Donny.  Not a bunch of fig-eaters with towels on their heads tryin' to find reverse on a Soviet tank.  This is not a worthy--

paragraphs.speaker: VOICE
paragraphs.text: HEY!
The Dude and Walter look.
Quintana is bellowing from the lip of the lane, and is restrained by O'Brien.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: What's this "day of rest" shit, man?!
Walter looks at him innocently.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: What is this bullshit, man?  I don't fucking care!  It don't matter to Jesus!  But you're not fooling me! You might fool the fucks in the league office, but you don't fool Jesus! It's bush league psych-out stuff! Laughable, man!  I would've fucked you in the ass Saturday, I'll fuck you in the ass next Wednesday instead!
He makes hip-grinding coital motions as O'Brien leads him away.

paragraphs.speaker: QUINTANA
paragraphs.text: You got a date Wednesday, man!
Walter, his head cocked, and the Dude, peeking over his shades, watch him go.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: He's cracking.
Donny, Walter and the Dude emerge from the alley, each holding his leatherette ball satchel.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: A tree of life, Dude.  To all who cling to it.
They react to the droning synthesizer-based technopop coming from a boom box.

Dieter, Kieffer and Franz, in shiny black leather, stand in a line facing them in the all-but-deserted lot.  Behind them orange flames lick gently at the Dude's car, which has been put to the torch.  The orange flames glow on the men's creaking leather.  Next to the car are three motorcycles, parked in a neat row.  The Dude looks sadly at the burning car.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: They finally did it.  They killed my fucking car.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: Vee vant zat money, Lebowski.

paragraphs.speaker: KIEFFER
paragraphs.text: Ja, uzzervize vee kill ze girl.

paragraphs.speaker: FRANZ
paragraphs.text: Ja, it seems you forgot our little deal, Lebowski.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You don't have the fucking girl, dipshits.  We know you never did. So you've got nothin' on my Johnson.
The men in black, stunned, confer amongst themselves in German.  Under his breath:

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: Are these the Nazis, Walter?
Walter answers, also sotto voce, his eyes still on the three men:

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: They're nihilists, Donny, nothing to be afraid of.
The Germans stop conferring.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: Vee don't care.  Vee still vant zat money or vee fuck you up.

paragraphs.speaker: KIEFFER
paragraphs.text: Ja, vee still vant ze money.  Vee sreaten you.
He pulls an uzi from under his coat.  It glints in the firelight.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Fuck you.  Fuck the three of you.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hey, cool it Walter.
Walter ignores the Dude, addresses the Germans:

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: There's no ransom if you don't have a fucking hostage.  That's what ransom is.  Those are the fucking rules.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: Zere ARE no ROOLZ!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: NO RULES!  YOU CABBAGE-EATING SONS- OF- BITCHES--

paragraphs.speaker: KIEFFER
paragraphs.text: His girlfriend gafe up her toe!  She sought we'd be getting million dollars!  Iss not fair!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Fair!  WHO'S THE FUCKING NIHILIST HERE!  WHAT ARE YOU, A BUNCH OF FUCKING CRYBABIES?!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hey, cool it Walter.  Listen, pal, there never was any money.  The big Lebowski gave me an empty briefcase, man, so take it up with him.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: AND I'D LIKE MY UNDIES BACK!
The Germans confer again, in German.
Donny is visibly frightened.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: Are they gonna hurt us, Walter?

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: They won't hurt us, Donny.  These men are cowards.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: Okay.  Vee take ze money you haf on you und vee call it eefen.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Fuck you.
The Dude is digging into his pocket.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Come on, Walter, we're ending this thing cheap.
Walter's eyes, burning with hatred, are locked on Dieter's.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What's mine is mine.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Come on, Walter!.
Louder, to the Germans, as he looks in his wallet:

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Four dollars here!
He inspects the change in his palm.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Almost five!

paragraphs.speaker: DONNY
paragraphs.text: (tremulously) I got eighteen dollars, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: (grimly) What's mine is mine.
With a ring of steel, Dieter produces a glinting saber.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: VEE FUCK YOU UP, MAN!  VEE TAKE YOUR MONEY!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: (coolly) Come and get it.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: VEE FUCK YOU UP, MAN!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Come and get it.  Fucking nihilist.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: I FUCK YOU!  I FUCK YOU!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Show me what you got.  Nihilist. Dipshit with a nine-toed woman.
In a rage, Dieter charges.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: I FUCK YOU!  I FUCK YOU!
hurls his leather satchel.
Watching Dieter's charge, is caught off-guard.  The bowling ball thuds into his chest and lifts him off his feet.
He falls back, his uzi clattering away.
twists away as Dieter reaches him; grabs Dieter's head in both hands; draws Dieter's head up to his mouth, which closes on Dieter's ear.

He rushes Franz but draws up short as Franz sends out karate kicks, his leather pants squeaking and popping.  Franz gives a loud cry with each kick; the Dude leans back, throwing his arms up, evading the kicks.
His jaw is still clamped on Dieter's ear.  Dieter draws his saber against Walter's side, drawing blood.
Walter doesn't react to the wound.  Growling as Dieter screams, he worries his ear, waggling his head with his jaws clamped.
Dieter drops it.
Awkwardly circling, evading Franz's kicks.
still worrying the ear.  With a tearing sound his head and Dieter's separate.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: I FUCK YOU!  YOU CANNOT HURT ME!  I BELIEF IN NUSSING!
Walter spits his ear into his face.
The Dude and Franz, both now panting heavily, have yet to establish body contact.  Franz continues to kick.

paragraphs.speaker: FRANZ
paragraphs.text: VEAKLING!
draws back his fist.

paragraphs.speaker: DIETER
paragraphs.text: NUSSING!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: ANTI-SEMITE!
Bam!--A powerhouse blow to the middle of his face drops Dieter for the count.
With a piercing shriek Franz finally summons the nerve to charge the Dude, hands raised to deliver karate blows.
As he reaches the Dude--WHHAP--the  boom box swings into frame to smash him in the face.  Its volume shoots up.
Walter bashes him a few more times over the head.  The music screeches to static, then quiet.  Laid out now, Franz too is quiet.
All quiet.
Walter, panting, looks around.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: We've got a man down, Dude.
With a hand pressed to his bleeding side he trots over to Donny, who lies gasping on the ground.
The Dude, also panting, rises and trots over.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hy God!  They shot him, Walter!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: No Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: They shot Donny!
Donny gasps for air.  His eyes, wide, go from the Dude to Walter.  One hand still clutches his eighteen dollars.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: There weren't any shots.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Then what's...

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: It's a heart attack.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Wha.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Call the medics, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Wha. . . Donny--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Hurry Dude.  I'd go but I'm pumping blood.  Might pass out.
The Dude runs into the lanes.  Walter lays a reassuring hand on Donny's shoulder.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Rest easy, good buddy, you're doing fine.  We got help choppering in.
They sit side by side, forearms on knees, in a nondescript waiting area.  Walter bounces the fingertips of one hand off those of the other.  They sit.  They wait.
A tall thin man in a conservative black suit enters.  He eyes the Dude's bowling attire and sunglasses and Walter's army surplus, but doesn't make an issue of it.

paragraphs.speaker: MAN
paragraphs.text: Hello, gentlemen.  You are the bereaved?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah man.

paragraphs.speaker: MAN
paragraphs.text: Francis Donnelly.  Pleased to meet you.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Jeffrey Lebowski.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Walter Sobchak.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: The Dude, actually.  Is what, uh.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Excuse me?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Nothing.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Yes.  I understand you're taking away the remains.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: We have the urn.
He nods through a door.  Another man in a black suit enters to carefully deposit a large silver urn on the desktop.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: And I assume this is credit card?
He is vaguely handing a large leather folder across the desk to whomever wants to take it.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah.

He takes it, opens it, puts on reading glasses that sit halfway down his nose, and inspects the bill with his head pulled back for focus and cocked for concentration.  Silence. The Dude smiles at Donnelly.  Donnelly gives back a mortician's smile.  At length Walter holds the bill towards Donnelly, pointing.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: What's this?

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: That is for the urn.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Don't need it.  We're scattering the ashes.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Yes, so we were informed.  However, we must of course transmit the remains to you in a receptacle.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: This is a hundred and eighty dollars.

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Yes sir.  It is our most modestly priced receptacle.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Well can we--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: A hundred and eighty dollars?!

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: They range up to three thousand.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Yeah, but we're--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Can we just rent it from you?

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Sir, this is a mortuary, not a rental house.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: We're scattering the fucking ashes!

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Walter--

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: JUST BECAUSE WE'RE BEREAVED DOESN'T MEAN WE'RE SAPS!

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: Sir, please lower your voice--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Hey man, don't you have something else you could put it in?

paragraphs.speaker: DONNELLY
paragraphs.text: That is our most modestly priced receptacle.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: GODDAMNIT!  IS THERE A RALPH'S AROUND HERE?!
It is a high, wind-swept bluff.  Walter and the Dude walk towards the lip of the bluff.  Parked in the background is one lonely car, Walter's.
Walter is carrying a bright red coffee can with a blue plastic lid.  When they reach the edge the two men stand awkwardly for a beat.  Finally:

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: I'll say a few words.
The Dude clasps his hands in front of him.  Walter clears his throat.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Donny was a good bowler, and a good man.  He was. . . He was one of us. He was a man who loved the outdoors, and bowling, and as a surfer explored the beaches of southern California from Redondo to Calabassos.  And he was an avid bowler.  And a good friend.  He died--he died as so many of his generation, before his time. In your wisdom you took him, Lord. As you took so many bright flowering young men, at Khe San and Lan Doc and Hill 364.  These young men gave their lives.  And Donny too.  Donny who. . . who loved bowling.
Walter clears his throat.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: And so, Theodore--Donald--Karabotsos, in accordance with what we think your dying wishes might well have been, we commit your mortal remains to the bosom of.
Walter is peeling the plastic lid off the coffee can.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: the Pacific Ocean, which you loved so well.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Goodnight, sweet prince.
paragraphs.direction: AS HE SHAKES OUT THE ASHES

paragraphs.text: The wind has blown all of the ashes into the Dude, standing just to the side of and behind Walter. The Dude stands, frozen. Finished eulogizing, Walter looks back.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shit, I'm sorry Dude.
He starts brushing off the Dude with his hands.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Goddamn wind.
Heretofore motionless, the Dude finally explodes, slapping Walter's hands away.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Goddamnit Walter!  You fucking asshole!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Dude!  Dude, I'm sorry!
The Dude is near tears.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You make everything a fucking travesty!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Dude, I'm--it was an accident!
The Dude gives Walter a furious shove.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: What about that shit about Vietnam!

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Dude, I'm sorry--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: What the fuck does Vietnam have to do with anything!  What the fuck were you talking about?!
Walter for the first time is genuinely distressed, almost lost.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Shit Dude, I'm sorry--

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: You're a fuck, Walter!
He gives Walter a weaker shove.  Walter seems dazed, then wraps his arms around the Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: WALTER
paragraphs.text: Awww, fuck it Dude.  Let's go bowling.

shots.heading: THE LANES THE DUDE AND WALTER BOWLING

paragraphs.text: We watch each of them glide across the floor, release, follow through--gracefully.  We have never seen them bowl before. They are quite good.  Each wears a black armband on his bowling shirt.
The Dude walks up to the bar.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Two oat sodas, Gary.

paragraphs.speaker: GARY
paragraphs.text: Right.  Good luck tomorrow.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Thanks, man.

paragraphs.speaker: GARY
paragraphs.text: Sorry to hear about Donny.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah.  Well, you know, sometimes you eat the bear, and, uh.
"Tumbling Tumbleweeds" has come up on the jukebox, and The Stranger ambles up to the bar.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: Howdy do, Dude.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Oh, hey man, how are ya?  I wondered if I'd see you again.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: Wouldn't miss the semis.  How things been goin'?

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Ahh, you know.  Strikes and gutters, ups and downs.
The Stranger's eyes crinkle merrily.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: Sure, I gotcha.
The bartender has put two gleaming beers on the counter.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Thanks, Gary...Take care, man, I gotta get back.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: Sure.  Take it easy, Dude--I know that you will.

paragraphs.speaker: DUDE
paragraphs.text: Yeah man.  Well, you know, the Dude abides.
Gazing after him, The Stranger drawls, savoring the words:

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: The Dude abides.
He gives his head a shake of appreciation, then looks into the camera.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: I don't know about you, but I take comfort in that.  It's good knowin' he's out there, the Dude, takin' her easy for all us sinners.  Shoosh.  I sure hope he makes The finals.  Welp, that about does her, wraps her all up.  Things seem to've worked out pretty good for the Dude'n Walter, and it was a purt good story, dontcha think?   Made me laugh to beat the band.  Parts, anyway.  Course--I didn't like seein' Donny go. But then, happen to know that there's a little Lebowski on the way.  I guess that's the way the whole durned human comedy keeps perpetuatin' it-self, down through the generations, westward the wagons, across the sands a time until-- aw, look at me, I'm ramblin' again.  Wal, uh hope you folks enjoyed yourselves.
He brushes his hat brim with a fingertip as we begin to pull back.

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: Catch ya further on down the trail.
As we pull away The Stranger swivels in to the bar.  As his voice fades:

paragraphs.speaker: THE STRANGER
paragraphs.text: ...Say friend, ya got any more a that good sarsaparilla?...
